question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a nodesEnvelope where all serviceEndpoint.DomainName values are set to domains with very long TTL-0 DNS records that resolve to different IPs on each lookup, causing the SDK to connect to unpredictable endpoints and potentially preventing gossip?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a nodesEnvelope where all serviceEndpoint entries have both DomainName and IPAddressV4 set to non-empty values, exploiting the DomainName-takes-precedence logic at lines 114-116 to ensure the attacker-controlled domain is used over the legitimate IP, redirecting gossip?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a nodesEnvelope where all serviceEndpoint.DomainName values are set to 'metadata.google.internal' or similar cloud metadata endpoints, causing the SDK to gossip transactions to cloud metadata services that return HTTP responses instead of gRPC, preventing consensus?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a nodesEnvelope where all serviceEndpoint.Port values are set to 22 (SSH), 3306 (MySQL), or other non-gRPC ports, causing the SDK to attempt gRPC handshakes with non-gRPC services that immediately close the connection, preventing gossip?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user inject a nodesEnvelope where all serviceEndpoint.DomainName values contain CNAME chains that ultimately resolve to attacker-controlled IPs, exploiting the lack of DNS pinning or DNSSEC validation to redirect gossip traffic?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/mirror_node_client.go -> Scope: High: Preventing gossip of a transaction or multiple transactions'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the HTTP client at line 46 has no redirect policy, allowing the mirror REST server to issue a 301/302 redirect to an attacker-controlled URL that serves a malicious nodesEnvelope, redirecting all gossip to attacker-controlled nodes?
